title: - - SELF REWARD DESIGN WITH FINE GRAINED INTER PRETABILITY [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - - SELF REWARD DESIGN WITH FINE GRAINED INTER PRETABILITY

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transparency and fairness issues in Deep Reinforcement Learning may stem from the black-box nature of deep neural networks used to learn its policy, value functions etc. This paper proposes a way to circumvent the issues through the bottomup design of neural networks (NN) with detailed interpretability, where each neuron or layer has its own meaning and utility that corresponds to humanly understandable concept. With deliberate design, we show that lavaland problems can be solved using NN model with few parameters. Furthermore, we introduce the Self Reward Design (SRD), inspired by the Inverse Reward Design, so that our interpretable design can (1) solve the problem by pure design (although imperfectly) (2) be optimized via SRD (3) perform avoidance of unknown states by recognizing the inactivations of neurons aggregated as the activation in _wunknown_ .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Deep Neural Network (DNN) have recently been integrated to solve problems with remarkable performance. The deep reinforcement learning greatly improves the state-of-the-art of control and, in the words of Sutton & Barto (2018), _learning from interaction_ . Among the well-known successes are (1) the Deep Q-Network (Mnih et al. (2015)) which enabled machine to play Atari Games with incredible performance, and (2) AlphaGo which is capable of playing notoriously complex game of Go (Silver et al. (2016)), which has also been further developed and popularized as being capable of defeating human at pro level. Although DNN has proven to possess great potentials, it is a blackbox that is difficult to interpret. To address this difficulty, various works have emerged, thus we have a host of different approaches to eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI); see surveys Arrieta et al. (2020); Gilpin et al. (2018); Tjoa & Guan (2020). They have shed some lights into the inner working of a DNN, but there may still be large gaps to fill. Note that there is no guarantee that interpretability is even attainable, especially when context-dependent interpretability can be subjective.

In this paper, we propose the Self Reward Design, a non-traditional RL solution that combines highly interpretable human-centric design and the power of DNN. Our robot (representing any artificial agent) rewards itself through purposeful design of DNN architecture, enabling it to solve problem **without training** . The solution might be sub-optimal, but the use of trainable DNN modules (we use pytorch, specifically) addresses the problem. We show that performance is improved while interpretability is retained.

This paper is arranged as the following. We start with clarifications. Then we briefly go through related works that inspire this paper. Then our interpretable design and SRD training idea are demonstrated with a 1D toy example, RobotFish. Following that, we introduce SRD on the main robot navigation problem on the 2D _lavaland_ with a large focus on interpretable design, followed by SRD training and unknown avoidance. Its experimental results will be discussed in the section after, and finally, we conclude with limitations and future works.

## 2 THIS PAPER FOCUSES HEAVILY ON INTERPRETABLE HUMAN DESIGN

**What exactly is this paper about?** Demonstrations of how two reinforcement learning problems are solved in an interpretable manner through self-reward mechanism. Readers will find how we

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

design different components taylored to different problem components. See appendix A.1 for more remarks: the paper has been heavily reorganized to direct readers’ focus on _interpretable design_ .

**But what is interpretability?** While there may be many ways to talk about interpretability, interpretability in the context of this paper is fine-grained, i.e. we go all the way down to _directly manipulating weights and biases_ of DNN modules. DNN modules are usually optimized using gradient descent from random initialization, thus the resulting weights are hard to interpret. In our SRD model, the meaning and purpose of each neural network component can be explicitly stated with respect to the environmental and model settings.

**How do we compare our interpretability** with existing explainable deep RL methods? Since we directly manipulate the weights and biases, our interpretability is at a very low level of abstraction, unlike post-hoc analysis e.g. saliency (Greydanus et al. (2018)) or semantically meaningful high level specification such as reward decomposition (Juozapaitis et al. (2019)). In other words, we aim to be as transparent as possible, allowing users to understand the model from the most basic unit.

**Baseline** . Our main result on 2D robot lavaland example achieves a high performance of approximately 90% accuracy with 10% randomness to allow for exploration. Hence, we believe comparing accuracy performance with other RL methods is irrelevant, and, most importantly, distracting from our focus of interpretability. If possible, _we want to compare the level of interpretability_ . Quantitative comparison is tricky, then how do we compare? What baseline to use? We have in fact answered this question in the previous paragraph: our interpretability is fine-grained as we directly manipulate weights and biases.

**Different design for different contexts** . There is probably no cookie-cutter framework for SRD. Solution design that is interpretable according to the above definition necessarily and heavily depends on the context, i.e. the shape of the system, thus we might need different formula of losses. This paper demonstrates how purposeful and deliberate designs work on two RL problems. In our robot fish example, the task is simply to survive, hence the model rewards actions that encourages survival e.g. “eat when hungry”. In 2D robot lavaland example, each tile is considered as a potential checkpoint to reach before reaching the final target, thus the model uses tile-based module that rewards the tile relative to other tiles: the preferred tile is scored higher.

## 2.1 RELATED WORKS: FROM RL TO DEEP RL TO SRD

**RL imperfect human design** . RL system can be set up by human manually specifying the rewards. Unfortunately, human design can easily be imperfect since the designers might not necessarily grasp the full extent of complex problem. For RL agents, designers’ manual specification of rewards are fallible, subject to errors and problems such as _negative side effect of a misspecified reward_ (Clark & Amodei (2016)) and _reward hacking_ (Russell & Norvig (2010)). Dylan’s _inverse reward design_ (IRD) paper (Hadfield-Menell et al. (2017)) addresses this problem directly: it allows a model to learn beyond what imperfect designers specify. An important component of our solution to imperfect designer problem is the implementation of _unknown avoidance_ , particularly _wunknown_ in our 2D robot. Also see appendix A.2 for Reward Design Problem (RDP).

**From RL to Deep RL** . Not only is human design fallible, it might be very difficult, especially for complex problems. In the introduction, we mention that Deep RL (DRL) solves this by combining RL and the power of DNN. However, DRL rewards have been produced by the black-box: they become difficult to understand and thus explainable DRL emerges to address that problem.

RL papers that address explainability/interptretability problems have been compiled in survey papers (eg. Heuillet et al. (2021); Puiutta & Veith (2020)). Saliency, a common XAI method, has been applied to visualize deep RL’s mechanism (Greydanus et al. (2018)). Relational deep RL uses a relational module that not only improves the agent’s performance on StarCraft II and Box-World, but also provides visualization on the attention heads useful for interpretability (Zambaldi et al. (2019)). Other methods to improve interpretability include reward decomposition, in which each part of the decomposable reward is semantically meaningful (Juozapaitis et al. (2019)); do refer to the survey papers for several other ingenious designs and investigations into the interpretability of deep RL.
**From DRL to SRD** . Our model is called _self reward_ design because our robot computes its own reward, similar to DRL computation of Q-values. However, human design is necessary to put con-

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

straints on how self-rewarding is performed so that interpretability is maintained. Human designer has the responsibility of understanding the problems, dividing the problems into smaller chunks and then finding the relevant modules to plug into the design in a fully interpretable way (see for example how we use convolution layer to create the _food location detector_ , section 2.1).

**Computational Efficiency of SRD** . The models only use modules from standard DNN modules such as convolution (conv), deconvolution (deconv) and fully-connected (FC) layers with few trainable parameters (e.g. only 180 parameters in Robot2NN). With proper choice of initial parameters, we can skip the long, arduous training and optimization processes that are usually required for deep learning to learn unseen concepts. We trade off the time spent on training algorithm with the time spent on human design, thus addressing what Kahn et al. (2018) called _sample inefficiency_ (the need for large dataset hence long training time) in a human-centric way.

## 2.2 OTHER RELEVANT CONCEPTS

**Self-supervised Learning** . DRL like Value Prediction Network (VPN, Oh et al. (2017)) is selfsupervised. Exploration-based RL algorithm is applied so that data are gathered real-time for training: the model thus optimizes its reward function. Our model is similar in this aspect. Unlike VPN, however, our interpretable design avoids all the abstraction of DNN. Our SRD training is also selfsupervised in the sense that we do not require datasets with ground-truth labels. Instead, we induce semantic bias via interpretable components to achieve the correct solutions. Also see appendix A.2.

**Imagination component** . Our pipeline includes several rollouts of possible future trajectories similar to existing RL papers that use imagination components, with differences as the following. Compared to Kalweit & Boedecker (2017) which optimizes towards target value ˆ _yi_ = _ri_ + _γQ[′] i_ +1[(heavily] abbreviated), SRD (1) is similar because agent updates on every imaginary sample available, but (2) has different, context-dependent loss computation. Also see appendix A.2 for more references.

## 3 ROBOT FISH: 1D TOY EXAMPLE

**Problem setting** . To broadly illustrate the idea, we use a one-dimensional model Fish1D with Fish Neural Network (FishNN) deliberately designed to survive the simple environment. Robot Fish1D has _energy_ which is represented by a neuron labelled _F_ . Energy diminishes over time. If the energy reaches 0, it dies. The environment is _env_ = [ _e_ 1 _, e_ 2 _, e_ 3] where _ei_ = 0 _._ 5 indicates there is a food at position _i_ and no food if _ei_ = 0. The fish is always located in the first block of _env_ , fig. 1(A). In this problem, ‘food here’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] which means the food is near the fish. Similarly, ‘food there’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 0] or _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _,_ 0 _._ 5], which means the food is somewhere ahead and visible. ‘No food’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _,_ 0].

**Fish1D’s Actions** . (1) ‘eat’: recover energy _F_ when there is food in its current position. (2) ‘move’: movement to the right. In our implementation, ‘move’ causes _env_ to be rolled left. If we treat the environment as an infinite roll tape and _env_ as fish vision’s on the 3 immediately visible blocks, then the food is available every 5 block.

Figure 1: Robot Fish setting. The fish is half full/hungry, _F_ = 0 _._ 5. Left: no food. Middle: food on _env_ 3, thus “food there” neuron lights up. Right: food on _env_ 1, thus “food here” neuron lights up.

**How to design an interpretable component of neural network?** First, we want the fish to be able to distinguish 3 scenarios previously defined: food here, food there and no food. Suppose we want a neuron that strongly activates when there is food nearby (name it _food here_ neuron, _fh_ ), another neuron that strongly activates when there is a food nearby (name it _food there_ neuron, _ft_ ), and we want to represent the no-food scenario as ‘neither _fh_ and _ft_ respond’. How do we design a layer with two neurons with the above properties? We use 1D convolution layer and _selective activation_ function as _σsa_ ( _x_ ) = _ϵ/_ ( _||x||_[2] + _ϵ_ ), as the following.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**The** _**fh**_ **and** _**ft**_ **neurons** . Define the activation of _fh_ neuron as _afh_ = _σsa_ [ _convfh_ ( _env_ )] where _convfh_ , a Conv1D with weight array _wfh_ = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] and bias _bfh_ = 0 _._ 5. When there is food near the fish, we get _yfh_ = _convfh_ ( _env_ ) = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _∗_ [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _−_ 0 _._ 5 = 0 where _∗_ denotes the convolution operator, so _afh_ = _σsa_ ( _yfh_ ) = 1. This is a strong activation of neuron, because, by design, the maximum value of selective activation function is 1. We are not done yet. Similar to _afh_ above, define _aft_ . The important task is to make sure that when ‘there is food there but NOT HERE’, _aft_ activates strongly but _afh_ does not. They are _wft_ = [0 _,_ 1 _,_ 1] _, bft_ = _−_ 0 _._ 5. Together, they form the first layer called the Food Location Detector (FLD). Generally, we have used

**Interpretable FishNN** . To construct the neural network responsible for the fish’s actions (eat or move), we need one last step: connecting the neurons plus fish’s internal state (energy) (altogether [ _afh, aft, F_ ]) to the action output vector [ _eat, move_ ] _≡_ [ _e, m_ ] through FC layer, as shown in fig. 2 blue dotted box. The FC weights are chosen meaningfully e.g. ‘eat when hungry and there is food’ and to avoid scenarios like ‘eat when there is no food’. This is interpretable through manual weight and bias setting.

Figure 2: FishNN architecture, _δ >_ 0 a small value.

**Is FishNN’s decision correct?** The prefrontal cortex (PFC) decides whether FishNN’s decision is correct or not. PFC is seen in fig. 2 green dotted box. The name ‘PFC’ is only borrowed from the neuroscience to reflect our idea that this part of FishNN is associated with internal goals and decisions, similar to real brain (Miller et al. (2002)). How do we construct PFC? First, define _threshold activation_ as _τ_ ( _x_ ) = _Tanh_ ( _LeakyReLU_ ( _x_ )). Then PFC is constructed deliberately in the same way FishNN is constructed in an interpretable way as the following.

First, aggregate states from FishNN into a vector _v_ 0 = [ _afh, aft, F, e, m_ ]. This will be the input to PFC. Then, _v_ 0 is processed using _convP F C_ followed by softmax and threshold activation. The choice of weights and biases can be seen in table 2 in appendix B. With this design, we achieve meaningful activations _v_ 1 = [ _e_ 1 _, m_ 1 _, ex_ ] as before. For example, _e_ 1 is activated when “there is food and the fish eats it when it is hungry”, i.e. _fh_ = 1 _, F <_ 1 and _e_ is activated relative to _m_ 1. The output of PFC is binary vector [ _True, False_ ] = [ _T, F_ ] obtained from passing _v_ 1 through a FC layer _FCP F C_ . In this implementation, we have designed the model such that the activation of any _v_ 1 neuron is considered a _True_ response; otherwise it is considered false. This is how PFC judges whether FishNN’s action decision is correct.

**Self reward optimization** . As seen above, fish robot has FishNN that decides on an action to take and the PFC that determines whether the action is correct. Is this system already optimal? Yes, if we are only concerned with the fish’s survival, since the fish will not die from hunger. However, it is not optimal with respect to average energy. We optimize the system through standard DNN backpropagation with the following self reward loss

_loss_ = _CEL_ ( _z, argmax_ ( _z_ )) (2)

where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and _z_ = Σ _[mem] i_ =1[[] _[T, F]_[]] _[i]_[is][the][accumulated][decision][over] _mem_ = 8 iterations to consider past actions (pytorch notation is used). The ground-truth used in the loss is _argmax_ ( _z_ ), computed by the fish itself: hence, self-reward design.

**Results** . Human design ensures survival i.e. problem is solved correctly. Initially, fish robot decides to move rather than eat food when _F ≈_ 0 _._ 5, but after SRD training, it will prefer to eat whenever

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

food is available, as shown in fig. 3. New equilibrium is attained: it does not affect the robot’s survivability, but fish robot will now survive with higher average energy.

Figure 3: (A) plot of energy ( _F_ , blue) and food availability (red) for untrained model. SRD-trained model at its early stage looks almost identical. (B) same as (A) but after 12000 SRD training iterations. (C) Average energy of SRD-trained fish model.

## 4 2D ROBOT IN LAVALAND

**The lavaland problem** . Like Dylan’s IRD paper, we use lavaland as the test bed. Given a map with either dirt (brown), grass (green), lava (red) and target (yellow) tiles and the robot’s starting point (blue x), as shown in _xattn_ of fig 4, the robot chooses to move either UP, DOWN, LEFT or RIGHT to reach the target. We treat lava as the “unrecognized tile” that the designer forgets to take into account (imperfect designer). Problem is considered solved if the target is reached within 36 steps. Robot should prefer not to step on grass tiles but must be designed to avoid lava tile at all cost.

Figure 4: Robot2NN and PFC. Robot2NN makes 2D robot’s action decisions. _xattn_ (the whole visible map) and _wself_ (the agent’s position) are the direct input to the model. In _v_ 1 _, v_ Σ, red values are positive (desirable), white zero and blue negative (not desirable). 2D robot’s PFC (green dotted box) resembles RobotFish’s PFC in their function of accumulating decision values. [ _wgrass_ ]14 _,_ [ _wgrass_ ]23 are examples of strong activations that are interpretable through tile-based module.

**Interpretable design** . Like fish robot, we manually choose weights and bias for 2D robot’s neural network to solve the problem. Convolutional layers etc are used by Robot2NN to score the tiles (red better, blue worse), i.e. robot creates a _favourability gradient_ . Robot then chooses action that generally moves it from blue to red regions.

**Notations** . ABA: approximate binary array, an array whose entries are expected to be _≈_ 0 or 1. _DSt[n]_[, or DeconvSeq, is the sequence of] _[ n]_[ deconvolutional layers for a tile type] _[ t]_[.][Deconvolutional] layer, or deconv, is a regular DNN module. Each deconv is followed by Tanh activation for normalization and non-linearity. Normalization (to magnitude 1) ensures that DeconvSeq compares action choices in relative terms. Tile _t_ denotes the name of a tile, e.g. grass, but it also denotes its [0 _,_ 1] normalized RGB value, e.g. for grass, _t_ = [0 _,_ 128 _,_ 0] _/_ 255 or an array of tile values (they should be

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

obvious from the context). _τrecog_ = 10 _[−]_[4] is the recognition threshold. Designer needs to specify _P_ = _{pt_ : _t_ = _target, grass, ...}_ . For now _P_ is the set of untrainable parameters, each _pt_ roughly acting as the factor for scaling the true reward, inevitably affecting the robot’s final preferences for or against different tiles. They induce the biases that designers input into the model in a simple, interpretable way. We also define the _unknown avoidance_ parameter _ua ≥_ 0.

**Interpretable tile-based modules** are designed to explicitly map robot’s response to each specific tile type. Get ~~A~~ BA() function computes the ABA for each tile type: _wt_ = _σsa ◦ µ_ [( _xattn − t_ )[2] ] where _µ_ [ _._ ] is the mean across RGB channel. This is reminiscent of eq. 1; the difference is, each neuron responds to a tile type at each _xattn_ coordinates. Neuron activations are computed as _wη_ where _η_ =target, grass or dirt shown in fig. 4, e.g. strong activation for grass detection occurs at [ _wgrass_ ]14.

**Unknown avoidance** . Like Dylan’s IRD, we have a reliable mechanism for unknown avoidance, the Boolean array _wunknown_ = [(1 _−_ Σ _twt_ ) _> τrecog_ ] to be treated as floating point numbers. From the formula, it can be seen that _wunknown_ aggregates the negation of known activations. The unknown in our case is the lava tile that the imperfect human designer ‘forgets’ to account for.

**Interpretable tile scores** . The score _v_ Σ will be used to decide what actions robot will take. It is computed as the following. First, we compute _v_ 1, whose components are [ _v_ 1] _t_ , as the following. For target tiles and “self” tiles (original positions), [ _v_ 1] _t_ = _ptDSt_[5][[] _[w][t]_[]][.][For any other named tiles,][we] also create the _favourability gradients_ based on the initial and target positions, so that, for example, a grass tile nearer the target can be valued more than a dirt tile. Thus,

Each deconv in DeconvSeq consists of 1 input and 1 output channel. It is fully interpretable through our manual selection of kernel weights: kernel has size 3 with center value 1 and side value 0 _._ 1 as seen in fig. 5(A). Fig. 5(B, C) show examples of trained weights. This choice of values is intended roughly to create a centred area of effect, where the center of the tile contributes most significantly to _wt_ ; see for example [ _v_ 1] _target_ in fig. 4. Assign _v_ Σ _←_ Σ _t_ [ _v_ 1] _t_ ; this value will be dynamically changed throughout each plan-making process.

Figure 5: (A) Initial parameters; all deconvs in all DeconvSeq are initialized to the same 3x3 weights with center max value of 1 and 0.1 elsewhere. (B) Once trained, variations of weights are observed for Project A expt 1 (all 180 parameters are shown). (C) Similar to (B) but for project Compare A.

**Making one plan** . Each plan is a series of action, and each action is chosen as the following. Set the specific values for the target tile and tile of the original position to _v_ 0 and _−v_ 0 respectively, where _v_ 0 = _max{|v_ Σ _|}_ is computed without backpropagation gradient to prevent any complications. Finally, to incorporate lava avoidance, or generally avoidance of anything previously unseen, _v_ Σ _← v_ Σ _∗_ (1 _− wunknown_ )+ _−ua ∗ v_ 0 _∗ wunknown_ . From the current position, robot’s neighbouring [ _v_ Σ] _i_ where _i_ = _up, down, left, right_ values are collected, and neighbours with the top two values are chosen. From the top two choices, randomly choose one of them with a 9 to 1 odds, favouring the action with higher value. The randomness is to encourage exploration. After each action, the tile the robot leaves will be assigned 0 _._ 9 _v_ 0 to prevent the robot from oscillating back and forth, where _v_ 0 is separately computed inside step ~~u~~ pdate() function. A series of actions are chosen in this manner until the target is reached or a maximum of 36 iterations are reached. Thus we have obtained a _plan_ and its score _vplan_ = _N_ 1 _ξ_[Σ] _[i,j][∈][ξ]_[[] _[v]_[Σ][]] _[i,j]_[, the mean of all values assigned to the chosen tiles where] _[ ξ]_[ is] the trajectory (see fig. 4, orange arrows in _v_ Σ).

**Imagining multiple plans for SRD optimization** . Robot makes plans by imagining _nplans_ = 4 different trajectories to reach the target. It plans and executes the plan with the highest _vplan_ . Like

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Kalweit & Boedecker (2017), we use all _nplans_ imagination branches for training (SRD optimization) with possibly novel loss minimization:

where _N_ [ _._ ] normalizes _vplan_ to the magnitude of 1, where normalization factor is computed without gradient to prevent complication. Thus, robot’s PFC always undervalues its reward relative to an abstract, ideal value 1. The intended effect is to always try to maximize _vplan_ . A standard Stochastic Gradient Descent with learning rate 10 _[−]_[4] is used for optimization. This resembles fish’s PFC true or false neurons used for rewarding itself, except it is continuous.

## 5 EXPERIMENT AND RESULTS

**Experimental setup and comparison** . We experiment on several initial settings as the following. For each experiment, we randomly generate and save 4096 maps to evaluate the performance of both standard design and SRD trained model, and similarly 4096 maps for SRD training. Note: All codes are included, including codes for creating animated gifs of robot traversing the lavaland. See appendix C.1 regarding multiple trials of the same experiments we conducted for reproducibility (project B, C, D etc).

**Reaching targets without training, optimized by training** . Table 1 shows that even without training (“No SRD” columns), our interpretable designs have enabled relatively high rate of problem solving. With SRD, the accuracies are further improved.

Table 1: Accuracy comparisons for Project A, project Compare A, project With-Lava-A and project Lava NOAV A. _Acc_ = _nreached target/_ 4096. “No SRD” indicates no training: reasonable accuracy is attainable purely with design. SRD training generally increases the accuracies. Starred values reflect failure modes, matching their respective histograms; see fig. 6 and 7.

**Project A: standard design and SRD on maps without lava tiles** . The fraction of grass tiles is 0 _._ 3. No robot will be spawned at the target immediately. Here, we use _pdirt,grass_ = 0 _._ 2 _, −_ 0 _._ 8 chosen empirically. For training, we only run through 1 epoch, taking a very short time to complete (less than 0.5 hour per project without GPU). Fig. 6(B) shows the cumulative histogram of the number of tiles traversed with _ptarget_ = 2.

**Compare A: model is more focused on the target** . This setting is similar to Project A, but we use a more extreme parameter setting, i.e. set _ptarget_ = 10. The local reward of the target tile is increased, and from table 1, we see that this generally increases the accuracies of both standard and SRD trained model (more likely to reach the target tile). The value seems to produce more unstable results as well, i.e. we empirically observe more failure modes.

**Project Lava A** . In this experiment, 0 _._ 1 of the tiles randomly assigned as lava and unknown avoidance _ua_ = 2. We also set _ptarget_ = 10 thus, similar to _Compare A_ we see higher accuracy but some failure modes as well. The model is designed to not recognize a lava tile, so a lava tile will activate the robot’s Robot2NN _wunknown_ at lavas’ positions. Fig. 7(A) is just an example of how robot successfully avoids lava, while fig. 7(C) lava cumulative histograms show that there are nearly zero lava tiles traversed.

**Project Lava NOAV A** . Project Lava NOAV A is similar to Project Lava A, but we set _ua_ = 0, i.e. no unknown avoidance. The results are clear, we see in 7(D) that robot will traverse the lava tiles without much regards.

**Robot2NN weights and preserved interpretability** . This model is very efficient because it consists of only 180 trainable parameters, as shown fully in fig. 5(B,C). As expected of relatively simple

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 6: (A) A sample trajectory from Project A. (B) Cumulative histogram of the no. of tiles traversed by robot in Project A. Steeper histogram indicates that less tiles of the type are being traversed (for grass, steeper is better) (C) A sample trajectory from Compare A. (D) Similar to B, but for robot in Compare A.

Figure 7: (A) A sample trajectory from Lava A. (B) A sample trajectory from Lava NOAV A. A lava tile is traversed. (C) Cumulative histogram of the no. of tiles traversed by robot in Lava A. Almost zero lava tiles are traversed. (D) Similar to (B) but for Lava NOAV A, in which some lava tiles are traversed.

problems, there is no need for millions of parameters required to achieve high accuracy. High performance 90% accuracy is attained, given 10% randomness is allowed. The weights of target deconv appear to have been trained towards higher positive values (redder). The center value remains the most prominent for all, thus preserving our interpretability. Looking into individual variations, fig. 5(B) shows the weights from Robot2NN model of project A expt 1 while fig. 5(C) from project Compare A expt 1. The difference in grass weights are apparent. See appendix C.2 on (1) preserved interpretability (2) why no conclusion should be drawn from this observation.

## 6 CONCLUSION, LIMITATION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

_Generalizability and scalability_ . An important limitation to this design is the possible difficulty in creating specific design for very complex problems, for example, computer vision problems with high-dimensional state space. Problems with multitude of unknown variables might be difficult to factor into the system, or, if they are factored in, the designers’ imperfect understanding of the variables may create a poor model. Future works on applying interpretable SRD on complex systems might push Deep RL community into designing systems that aim not only to attain high performance, but also to be as highly interpretable as our design. **Controlling Weights and Biases** . A

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

future study on regularizing weights may be interesting. While we have shown that our design provides a good consistency between the interpretable weights before and after training, it is not surprising that the combination of small differences in weights can yield different final weights that still perform well. So far, it is not clear how different parameters affect the performance of a model, or if there are any ways to regularize the training of weight patterns towards something more interpretable and consistent. So far, there is no guarantee for convergence either, and it may be difficult to provide a general proof, since the design is heavily dependent on the context. A **technical limitation** is the lack of APIs that perform the exact operations we may need to parallelize the imaginative portions of SRD optimization.

**Conclusion** . Finally, we have demonstrated interpretable neural network design with fine-grained interpretability. Human experts purposefully design models that solve specific problems based on their knowledge of the problems. SRD is introduced as a way to improve performance on top of the designers’ imperfection. The system is highly interpretable with manual adjustment of weights and biases. It is also efficient since very few weights are needed compared to traditional DNN.

## ETHICS STATEMENTS

There is no specific ethical statement present. We demonstrate methods for designing neural networks for RL problem in a transparent way. We believe each part is transparently presented, and, if the paper is insufficient, the code in supp. materials should fully complements each description made in this paper.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENTS

All codes are available in the supp. materials, to be released to public repository in case of acceptance. Results are tested on different random number seeds, and irregularities or mode collapses have been presented clearly in the paper as well (see for example the annotations made on the cumulative histograms).

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to 'white-box' reinforcement learning by manually designing neural network weights to encode human heuristics, which are then refined via self-supervised optimization. While the concept of fine-grained interpretability through manual weight initialization is novel and the results on simple grids (lavaland) are encouraging, the technical exposition suffers from several mathematical inconsistencies, lack of formal definitions for custom activation functions, and underspecified training procedures that would make reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Unspecified Selective Activation Function
comments.quote: \sigma_{sa}(x) = \epsilon / (||x||_2 + \epsilon)
comments.explanation: The authors define a 'selective activation' function to identify specific inputs (like food or tile colors). However, the input 'x' to this function is not clearly defined in the context of the convolution. In standard CNNs, the output of a convolution at a point is a scalar, but the use of the L2 norm suggests 'x' might be a vector of differences or a patch. Furthermore, the value of \epsilon is never specified. Since \sigma_{sa} is central to their 'detection' mechanism (Equations 1 and the FLD), the lack of detail makes the core architecture hard to reproduce.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Convolutional Weight Dimensions
comments.quote: w_{fh} = [1, 0, 0] and bias b_{fh} = 0.5. When there is food near the fish, we get y_{fh} = conv_{fh}(env) = [1, 0, 0] * [0.5, 0, 0] - 0.5 = 0
comments.explanation: The math here is slightly inconsistent with standard convolution definitions. If the weight is [1, 0, 0] and the input is [0.5, 0, 0], the dot product is 0.5. To get y=0 with a bias of 0.5, the bias must be subtracted (y = Wx - b), but standard NN notation is y = Wx + b. Later in Eq 1, the authors use b = -0.5. This sign inconsistency between the prose example and the formal definition is confusing. Additionally, 'env' is defined as a 3-element vector, and the kernel is also size 3, making this a full-input dot product rather than a sliding convolution, which isn't explicitly clarified.

comments.title: Underspecified Favourability Gradient Calculation
comments.explanation: The paper explains that a sequence of deconvolutional layers (DeconvSeq) is used to create a 'favourability gradient' so the robot moves toward the target. However, the number of layers (n) and how these gradients actually propagate 'value' across the grid is vague. While Figure 5 shows 3x3 kernels, it is not mathematically explained how a few layers of 3x3 deconvolutions transform a single 'target' activation into a global gradient that covers a 2D map. If the map is large, a sequence of 5 kernels (as implied by DS_t^{(5)}) would only have a receptive field of 11x11.

comments.title: Mathematical Inconsistency in Loss Function
comments.quote: loss = N[1 - v_{plan}]
comments.explanation: The authors define a loss to maximize the plan score. However, they state that v_{plan} is normalized to magnitude 1 (N[.]), but the normalization factor is computed without gradient. If N[v_{plan}] is always forced to 1, the gradient of (1 - 1) is zero, and no learning would occur. I suspect the authors mean they normalize the *target* or use a specific reference value, but as written, the formula suggests the loss would be a constant zero or behave unpredictably depending on what N[.] refers to.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in Experiments
comments.quote: Project Lava A ... accuracy but some failure modes ... Compare A ... 0.589* / 0.422*
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge 'failure modes' in table 1, but do not provide an adequate technical explanation for why setting p_{target}=10 (which should theoretically make the goal more attractive) causes the accuracy to drop from ~86% to ~42%. In a designed system where the logic is supposed to be fine-grained and interpretable, such a massive collapse in performance upon scaling a positive parameter suggests an underlying instability in the SRD training loop or the 'favourability gradient' logic that isn't addressed.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents an innovative and thought-provoking approach to interpretable deep reinforcement learning through Self Reward Design (SRD), emphasizing fine-grained control over network components. The core idea—designing interpretable NN modules with human-understandable neurons and leveraging self-supervised optimization—is well-motivated and demonstrated on two intuitive problems: a 1D robot fish survival task and a 2D robot navigation task in 'lavaland'. The authors convincingly show how deliberate weight and bias design can lead to interpretable solutions without training, later improved by SRD. While the conceptual framework and empirical results are promising, there are several technical issues concerning mathematical consistency, notation clarity, and methodological underspecification, particularly in the 2D model’s scoring and deconvolution mechanics. Some claims about performance and interpretability preservation after training could be more carefully qualified. Nevertheless, the vision of transparent, design-first RL is compelling and contributes meaningfully to the XAI literature.

comments.title: Incorrect convolution computation in Fish1D example
comments.quote: When there is food near the fish, we get _yfh_ = _convfh_ ( _env_ ) = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _∗_ [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _−_ 0 _._ 5 = 0
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the convolution operation was incorrectly computed: [1, 0, 0] * [0.5, 0, 0] should yield 0.5 (dot product), and subtracting bias bfh = 0.5 gives yfh = 0, which matches the text. However, the notation '∗' is used for convolution, but in context, it appears to denote element-wise multiplication or dot product. Given wfh = [1,0,0] and env = [0.5,0,0], conv1d should compute the inner product at alignment position. But if 'rolled left' operation is simulated via shift, the indexing may offset. With env = [0.5,0,0] and wfh = [1,0,0], centering the filter on first position gives correct result. The computation as written relies on precise alignment. While the numerical result is correct under correct alignment assumption, the lack of formal convolution definition (e.g., stride, padding) introduces ambiguity. This is resolved contextually by the example, but problematic for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of selective activation function
comments.explanation: The stated selective activation σsa(x) = ε / (||x||₂ + ε) produces outputs in (0,1], decreasing with ||x||, so activation is high when input norm is low. But this contradicts the intended behavior: we want strong activation when food is present (i.e., higher input). For example, in 'food here', convfh(env) produces a non-zero output, yet σsa maps it to a small value. This suggests a sign or functional form error. If σsa is meant to activate on presence, a likely correction is σsa(x) = 1 - ε/(||x||₂ + ε) or use of inverse. However, in context, the authors state that with yfh = 0, σsa(0) = ε/ε = 1, so activation is high. This only works if yfh is *small*. But when food is present, yfh should be large. Contradiction arises: wfh=[1,0,0], env=[0.5,0,0] → yfh=0.5, then afh = ε/(||0.5|| + ε). For ε<<1, this is small. But authors claim afh=1. Only if ε≈0.5 and norm computed per-neuron? Ambiguity in norm application. The text claims afh=1 when yfh=0, which only holds if input to σsa is the *difference* or residual, not raw. But no such operation is stated. This indicates a formula error unless ||x|| is misinterpreted.

comments.title: Unclear aggregation in v_Σ computation
comments.quote: Assign _v_ Σ _←_ Σ _t_ [ _v_ 1] _t_ ; this value will be dynamically changed throughout each plan-making process.
comments.explanation: The notation Σ_t [v_1]_t suggests summing over tile types t, where each [v_1]_t is a spatial map from DeconvSeq. But later, _v_Σ is used as a spatial score map to select actions. The paper lacks clarity on how DeconvSeq outputs are combined. Is [v_1]_t a scalar or a map? From context, it's a map. Then summing Σ_t [v_1]_t yields a single composite score map. But DeconvSeq(t) processes w_t, which is already a map. However, it’s unclear how the deconvolution sequence (multiple layers) is applied or folded. The model uses DSt[5], implying 5 deconv layers per tile type. But no composition rule across layers is given. The omission of activation normalization and skip connections across deconv makes the design underspecified. The result is ambiguous for reproduction.

comments.title: Misleading claim about optimization eliminating training need
comments.quote: Our robot rewards itself through purposeful design of DNN architecture, enabling it to solve problem **without training** .
comments.explanation: While the design does allow functionality without initial training (i.e., zero gradient steps), the phrase 'without training' is misleading because SRD still requires backpropagation and optimization during the self-reward phase. The system is not static; it undergoes training via loss minimization. The intended point—that a functional policy can exist prior to learning—is valid, but the phrasing overstates it. A more precise statement is that a baseline policy is hand-designed, then refined via training. This is a common pattern in inverse RL or reward shaping, not a total bypass of training. Given the context of SRD optimization, the claim is too strong.

comments.title: Underspecified threshold activation function in PFC
comments.quote: define _threshold activation_ as _τ_ ( _x_ ) = _Tanh_ ( _LeakyReLU_ ( _x_ ))
comments.explanation: The PFC uses τ(x) = Tanh(LeakyReLU(x)), but this is never applied in a clear way. From the text: 'then PFC is processed using convPFC followed by softmax and threshold activation'. This suggests multiple activations applied sequentially. But softmax and Tanh are both nonlinearities—applying both lacks justification. Moreover, LeakyReLU before Tanh introduces asymmetry that Tanh alone would not. The purpose of this cascade is ambiguous: is τ used on intermediate features, logits, or outputs? The output of PFC is claimed to be [True, False], a binary decision, yet τ maps to [-1,1]. No thresholding (e.g., hard sign) is described. The use of 'threshold activation' as a name contradicts its smooth output. This combination could be meaningful (e.g., bounded nonlinearity), but without circuit details or output examples, it’s underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in plan selection and scoring mechanism
comments.quote: from the top two choices, randomly choose one of them with a 9 to 1 odds, favouring the action with higher value
comments.explanation: The ratio '9 to 1 odds' is ambiguous: does this mean probability 9/10 (0.9) for the higher, or 9:1 = 0.9 for the higher? It's likely the latter, but 'odds' vs 'probability' can confuse. More critically, the plan-making process scores trajectories via vplan = N_1 ξ[Σ_(i,j)∈ξ [v_Σ]_(i,j)], defined as the mean of values along the path. This mean could be dominated by negative zones (e.g., lava avoidance penalty), but no normalization across plan length is mentioned. Longer plans may accumulate lower means, biasing toward shorter paths, but the objective is to reach target within 36 steps. The scoring function does not explicitly encourage shorter paths. The claim that high vplan leads to better plans is plausible, but the lack of discussion on length normalization or completion time introduces ambiguity in optimization objective.

comments.title: Incorrect or ambiguous normalization in SRD loss
comments.quote: where _N_ [ _._ ] normalizes _vplan_ to the magnitude of 1
comments.explanation: The statement 'normalizes vplan to the magnitude of 1' is ambiguous: is this L1, L2, or max-normalization? The phrase 'magnitude of 1' suggests L2 normalization, but in context, vplan is a scalar (a mean). How can a scalar be normalized to have magnitude 1? One possibility is that N[.] is a scalar normalization (vplan / ||vplan||), reducing it to sign ±1. But this would nullify gradient scale. Alternatively, if N[.] normalizes multiple vplan values across imagined plans, the description doesn't specify that. The paper states: 'robot’s PFC always undervalues its reward relative to an abstract, ideal value 1'. This suggests N[.] = 1/||vplan|| or similar, but then N[vplan] = 1 implies vplan=1, conflicting with 'undervalues'. The mechanism remains underspecified, affecting reproducibility of training dynamics.

comments.title: Missing factor in unknown avoidance formulation
comments.quote: _wunknown_ = [(1 _−_ Σ _twt_ ) _> τrecog_]
comments.explanation: The formulation aggregates unknown by 1 − Σ_t w_t, where w_t is an ABA for each tile. But w_t = σsa ◦ µ[(xattn − t)^2], which is a soft indicator, not binary. So Σ_t w_t may exceed 1 even in known regions (if multiple tile responses overlap). Thus (1 − Σ_t w_t) may be negative everywhere, and the threshold test [(> τrecog)] becomes meaningless. To function as a Boolean for 'no known tile detected', the sum should be close to 1 where known and <1 elsewhere. But with continuous w_t ∈ [0,1], and possible overlap, Σ_t w_t can be >1. The claim that wunknown detects unrecognized tiles (like lava) fails unless w_t are sparse and non-overlapping. No mechanism prevents overlap. For example, if xattn is grass, w_grass ≈1, others small, sum ≈1. But if xattn is lava, and no w_lava, all w_t ≈0, sum≈0, so 1−sum≈1, triggering wunknown. So it works only if Σ_t w_t ≈ 0 on unknown tiles. But τrecog=10e-4 is very small, so even small activation may block detection. The design is plausible but sensitive to threshold choice and activation sparsity, which is not analyzed.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to interpretable RL through manually designed neural networks with semantic meaning attached to weights and biases. The core idea of Self Reward Design (SRD) is novel and the demonstrations on 1D and 2D problems are clear. However, there are several mathematical inconsistencies and underspecified methods that undermine the technical rigor. The convolution calculation in FishNN contains a clear error, the selective activation function usage is inconsistent, and the self-reward loss functions lack proper justification. While the concept of fine-grained interpretability is valuable, the paper could benefit from clearer specifications of the training procedures and more rigorous evaluation of how interpretability is preserved after training.

comments.title: Incorrect convolution calculation in FishNN
comments.explanation: The convolution of [1,0,0] with [0.5,0,0] should yield 0.5, not 0. The convolution operation ∗ typically computes the dot product of the kernel with the input window. Here, [1,0,0] dotted with [0.5,0,0] equals 0.5. After subtracting the bias 0.5, the result would be 0, but the intermediate step is incorrectly stated as 0. This makes the subsequent activation calculation unclear since σsa(0) = ε/(0 + ε) = 1, which contradicts the expected behavior when food is nearby.

comments.title: Inconsistent selective activation function usage
comments.quote: Define the activation of _fh_ neuron as _afh_ = _σsa_ [ _convfh_ ( _env_ )] where _convfh_ , a Conv1D with weight array _wfh_ = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] and bias _bfh_ = 0 _._ 5. When there is food near the fish, we get _yfh_ = _convfh_ ( _env_ ) = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _∗_ [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _−_ 0 _._ 5 = 0 where _∗_ denotes the convolution operator, so _afh_ = _σsa_ ( _yfh_ ) = 1.
comments.explanation: The selective activation function σsa(x) = ε/(||x||_2 + ε) is defined to output values in (0,1], with maximum 1 achieved when x=0. However, the text states that afh=1 when food is nearby, which would require yfh=0. But if yfh=0 (after bias subtraction), this means the convolution output before bias was 0.5, which doesn't align with the expectation that the neuron should strongly activate when food is present. The relationship between the convolution output and the semantic meaning of 'strong activation' is inconsistent.

comments.title: Underspecified self-reward loss function
comments.quote: _loss_ = _CEL_ ( _z, argmax_ ( _z_ )) (2)
comments.explanation: The self-reward loss function uses cross-entropy loss where both the prediction and target are derived from the same quantity z. This is unconventional - typically CE loss compares predictions with ground-truth labels. The paper doesn't explain why argmax(z) is used as the target or how this leads to meaningful learning. The definition of z as Σ_[mem] i=1[[T,F]][i] (accumulated decisions over mem=8 iterations) is also unclear - how are the True/False values accumulated into a scalar z for CE loss?

comments.title: Ambiguous deconvolution sequence specification
comments.quote: For target tiles and "self" tiles (original positions), [ _v_ 1] _t_ = _ptDSt_[5][[] _[w][t]_[]][.][For any other named tiles,][we] also create the _favourability gradients_ based on the initial and target positions
comments.explanation: The notation DSt[n] (DeconvSeq) is defined as a sequence of deconvolutional layers, but the exact architecture and how it processes w_t is unclear. The subscript [5] appears but is not explained. The formula [v_1]_t = ptDSt[w_t] lacks mathematical precision - is this matrix multiplication, convolution, or some other operation? Without clearer specification, reproducing this component would be difficult.

comments.title: Questionable interpretability preservation claim
comments.quote: Looking into individual variations, fig. 5(B) shows the weights from Robot2NN model of project A expt 1 while fig. 5(C) from project Compare A expt 1. The difference in grass weights are apparent.
comments.explanation: The paper claims interpretability is preserved after training, but only shows that weights remain centered with positive values. However, the semantic meaning of specific weight patterns (e.g., why grass weights differ between experiments) is not analyzed. Without a systematic evaluation of how the trained weights maintain their intended semantic meaning, the claim of preserved interpretability is not substantiated. The appendix note that 'no conclusion should be drawn from this observation' further weakens this claim.

comments.title: Unexplained failure modes in Table 1
comments.quote: Starred values reflect failure modes, matching their respective histograms; see fig. 6 and 7.
comments.explanation: The starred accuracy values (e.g., 0.589* and 0.422* for Compare A SRD) are mentioned to reflect failure modes but these failures are not described in the text. The histograms in figures 6 and 7 show cumulative distributions but don't clearly indicate what constitutes a failure mode. Without explaining these failures, the reader cannot understand the limitations or instability of the SRD training process.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel approach to reinforcement learning called Self Reward Design (SRD), which emphasizes human-interpretable design of neural network parameters over black-box training. While the core idea of 'design-first' neural architectures is compelling and well-motivated within the context of interpretability, the paper suffers from significant mathematical ambiguity and underspecified methodology. The notation is frequently inconsistent, and key mechanics—specifically the derivation of the loss functions and the exact interaction between the 'Self Reward' optimization and the hard-coded architectural biases—are often described in a way that makes reproduction difficult. The paper would benefit from a more formal mathematical grounding to support its claims of 'interpretable' optimization.

comments.title: Ambiguous Loss Function Definition
comments.quote: where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and $z = \sum_{[mem]} i=1 [] [T, F] [] [i] [is][the][accumulated][decision][over] mem = 8$ iterations
comments.explanation: The definition of $z$ is highly fragmented due to apparent OCR artifacts and non-standard notation. It is unclear how the [T, F] vector (representing True/False judgments from the PFC) is being summed or what the index $i$ signifies in relation to the memory buffer. Furthermore, using Cross Entropy Loss on this aggregated 'memory' of judgments is conceptually non-obvious, as the relationship between the categorical output of a classifier (CEL base case) and a sequence of previous binary decisions is not formally justified.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Selective Activation Function
comments.explanation: The author defines the selective activation function $\sigma_{sa}(x)$ as $\epsilon / ( ||x||_2 + \epsilon )$. In standard neural network design, such a function would map high inputs to near-zero values and inputs near zero to approximately 1 (if $\epsilon$ is small). However, the text later asserts: 'the maximum value of selective activation function is 1' and 'strong activation of neuron... $afh = 1$'. If $x$ (the result of the convolution) is large, $\sigma_{sa}$ will be close to 0, which contradicts the stated goal of using this function to create 'strong activation' for detection. The math seems inverted relative to the claimed intent.

comments.title: Underspecified Unknown Avoidance Mechanism
comments.quote: $w_{unknown} = [(1 - \sum_t w_t) > τ_{recog}] $
comments.explanation: The expression uses a sum of weights $w_t$ across different tile types to define 'unknown' states. It is entirely unclear how the values of $w_t$ are normalized or bounded. If $\sum w_t$ exceeds 1, $w_{unknown}$ could be negative or behave erratically. The paper fails to define the domain of $w_t$ or provide the necessary constraints to ensure that this boolean indicator functions as intended. Without these boundary conditions, the 'unknown avoidance' mechanism is not reproducible.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Update Rule
comments.quote: $v_{\Sigma} ← v_{\Sigma} * (1 - w_{unknown}) + -ua * v_0 * w_{unknown}$
comments.explanation: The symbol '$-ua$' is confusing. In standard notation, a negation sign before a parameter usually implies subtraction. Given the context (avoidance of lava), the author likely intends a penalty term. However, the syntax '--' or '$-ua$' could be interpreted as a typo (e.g., $ua$ vs $-ua$). If the intention is to subtract a penalty proportional to $ua$, the sign is likely correct, but the presentation as '$-ua * v_0 * w_{unknown}$' in a line of code-like logic is visually ambiguous and prone to misinterpretation.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel, highly interpretable approach to reinforcement learning by manually constructing neural network components and refining them via a self-reward mechanism. The core idea of bottom-up, human-centric design is compelling and well-motivated for the explainability domain. However, the methodology section contains critical gaps, including missing equations for the tile-scoring gradients and the SRD optimization loss, which severely hinders reproducibility. Additionally, several mathematical formulations and initialization steps contain logical circularity or ambiguous notation that conflict with standard deep learning and RL conventions. While the toy example and experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of the approach, the paper would benefit from rigorous mathematical clarification, explicit specification of unimplemented loss terms, and clearer notation to align with the claims of fine-grained interpretability.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interpretable approach to deep RL by manually designing neural network weights and biases. The core idea of Self Reward Design (SRD) is interesting, but the exposition suffers from several critical gaps and ambiguities that hinder reproducibility. The most serious issue is a missing equation for the tile-based scoring mechanism in the 2D lavaland problem, which is central to the method. Additionally, loss functions for both the 1D and 2D examples are inadequately specified, and the construction of the PFC component is described too vaguely. While the paper successfully demonstrates the feasibility of high interpretability with few parameters, these omissions make it difficult for a reader to fully understand or replicate the work without the provided code.

comments.title: Missing formula for tile-based scoring in 2D lavaland
comments.quote: Thus,
comments.explanation: After the sentence ending with 'Thus,' (at the bottom of page 6), the paper appears to be cut off. The surrounding text says that for non-target tiles, 'favourability gradients' are created, but the actual formula for [v1]_t for 'any other named tiles' is never provided. This is a critical gap because the scoring mechanism (v1 and vΣ) is the core of the interpretable design and the basis for action selection. Without this equation, the method is underspecified and cannot be reproduced from the text alone. This might be an OCR artifact where the LaTeX equation was lost, but it remains a significant omission.

comments.title: Unclear loss function for SRD optimization in 2D lavaland
comments.quote: where N[.] normalizes vplan to the magnitude of 1, where normalization factor is computed without gradient to prevent complication.
comments.explanation: The paper states that a novel loss minimization is used for SRD training, but the actual loss function is never explicitly written. The description only mentions normalizing vplan to magnitude 1 and using standard SGD. It is unclear whether the loss is (N(vplan) - 1)^2, or something like -vplan, or a cross-entropy between scores and target. The vagueness prevents a reader from understanding how the optimization works, and it is a key part of the training method. This is a moderate issue that should be clarified.

comments.title: Ambiguous construction of the PFC in the 1D Fish example
comments.quote: Then PFC is constructed deliberately in the same way FishNN is constructed in an interpretable way as the following. First, aggregate states from FishNN into a vector v0 = [afh, aft, F, e, m]. This will be the input to PFC. Then, v0 is processed using convPFC followed by softmax and threshold activation. The choice of weights and biases can be seen in table 2 in appendix B. With this design, we achieve meaningful activations v1 = [e1, m1, ex] as before.
comments.explanation: The description of the PFC construction is too high-level. The threshold activation τ(x) = Tanh(LeakyReLU(x)) is defined earlier but it is not clear where it is applied (before softmax? after?). The roles of convPFC and the softmax are not explained. Additionally, the output of PFC is said to be a binary vector [T, F] obtained from passing v1 through a FC layer, but the mapping from v1 to binary is unclear: 'the activation of any v1 neuron is considered a True response; otherwise false' – how is 'activation' defined? Without precise details, the decision process of the PFC is ambiguous. This undermines the claim of fine-grained interpretability for that component.

comments.title: Self-reward loss for Fish1D appears circular
comments.quote: loss = CEL(z, argmax(z)) where z = Σ_{i=1}^{mem} [[T,F]]_i is the accumulated decision over mem = 8 iterations to consider past actions (pytorch notation is used). The ground-truth used in the loss is argmax(z), computed by the fish itself: hence, self-reward design.
comments.explanation: The loss uses the network's own aggregated output as the target (argmax(z)). This is essentially a form of self-training where the network is trained to predict its own decisions, which could lead to trivial solutions (e.g., always outputting the same decision without improvement). The paper claims this optimizes the fish's behavior (e.g., preferring to eat when hungry), but it is not explained why this loss drives the fish towards higher average energy. The accumulation over 8 steps might add temporal consistency, but the reasoning is insufficient. The mechanism by which this loss improves performance needs more justification or a different formulation. This is a moderate concern about the validity of the optimization.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a novel 'Self Reward Design' (SRD) framework that emphasizes fine-grained interpretability by manually designing neural network architectures and weights before training, supplemented by a training mechanism similar to imagination-augmented agents. The core idea—replacing standard reward engineering with architecture design that is then refined via SRD—is valuable for the XAI in RL community. However, the paper contains significant issues. Specifically, the experimental claims contradict the provided table data (some SRD experiments drastically reduce accuracy). Furthermore, the mathematical formulation in the Lavaland section is dimensionally inconsistent, treating the variable `v_sum` first as a scalar sum and then as a vector field without clear definition or conversion. While the Fish example demonstrates the concept well, these errors undermine the reliability of the claims regarding the efficacy of the SRD method in complex environments.

comments.title: Questionable claims regarding SRD performance improvements
comments.quote: SRD training generally increases the accuracies.
comments.explanation: I initially focused on the claim that SRD improves performance based on the narrative of the paper. However, checking Table 1 reveals a significant discrepancy. In Project Compare A, Experiment 2 and 4, the 'No SRD' accuracy is 87.1% and 86.3% respectively, while the 'SRD' accuracy drops to 58.9% and 42.2% (marked with asterisks for failure modes). Even though the text mentions 'generally', the specific failure modes are severe drops in performance. The authors should qualify the claim to reflect this instability or provide more analysis on why these specific 'failure modes' (which seem systematic) occur in the SRD variant.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in Lavaland tile update logic
comments.quote: Assign the specific values for the target tile and tile of the original position to v_0 and -v_0, where v_0 = max{|v_sum|} is computed without backpropagation gradient... v_sum ← v_sum ∗ (1 − wunknown) + −ua ∗ v_0 ∗ wunknown. From the current position, robot's neighbours [v_sum]_i where i = up, down, left, right values are collected...
comments.explanation: I found a major conceptual and notational conflict in the 'Making one plan' section. `v_sum` is initially defined as a scalar: `v_Σ ← Σ_t [v_1]_t` (Equation following the DeconvSeq description). However, later in the same paragraph, the text treats `v_sum` as a vector field that can be assigned specific values (like `0.9 v_0` to a tile) and, crucially, collected from neighbours (`[v_sum]_i`). The formula `v_sum ← v_sum ∗ (1 - wunknown)` implies a vector operation where a scalar is scaled by a mask, or a vector is element-wise multiplied. Without explicit clarification that `v_sum` mutates from a scalar to a 2D tensor of tile scores (or that the update is broadcast from a scalar `v_0`), the logic for how the robot selects 'top two neighbours' is difficult to follow or may be mathematically ill-defined.

comments.title: Ambiguous loss function specification for Fish SRD
comments.quote: loss = CEL(z, argmax(z))
comments.explanation: The definition of the loss function in Section 3 is mathematically ambiguous. The text states `z = [T, F]` is the accumulated decision over `mem = 8` iterations. If `z` is interpreted as a list of past decisions (T/F), `argmax` is not defined in the standard context (usually applies to probability vectors or lists of numbers). Even if `z` is the final 2-vector `[T, F]`, `argmax(z)` returns a class index, which requires `z` to be the input logits (or probabilities) to `CrossEntropyLoss`. The description 'accumulated decision' is vague; is it a list of indices, a probability distribution, or a raw value? The formula as written conflates these possibilities.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter notation
comments.quote: we use pdirt,grass = 0.2, −0.8... set ptarget = 10
comments.explanation: While scanning the 'Experimental setup' section, I noticed the notation for the parameters `p_t` used in the definition is inconsistent with their usage in the experiments. In the general definition, parameters are denoted `p_t` (where `t` denotes tile type like grass, target). However, in the specific experimental descriptions, the authors use `pdirt` (lowercase d) and `ptarget` (lowercase t), which is inconsistent with the standard notation `p_grass` or `p_target` that might be expected. This makes it slightly harder to map the specific experimental settings back to the formal definitions provided earlier.
